title:
House - Government, Elections And Indian Affairs (Room 305)
House Government, Elections And Indian Affairs
Adjourned
2026-02-18 09:34-10:13
committee: House Government, Elections and Indian Affairs

bills_discussed.title: Technical Assistance for Tribal Capital Outlay Projects
Health and Firearm Safety Research Task Force

summary: The committee heard two memorials. HM 64, which requests technical assistance for tribal entities regarding capital outlay projects, passed unanimously. HM 65, which seeks to establish a task force to study firearm violence as a public health issue, passed on a 4-3 party-line vote following debate over the inclusion of firearm safety instructors and certain data points in the memorial's preamble.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Good morning. Let's go ahead and allow our attendees to come in. The meeting is called to order. Clerk, please follow with the roll call.

turns.speaker: Committee Clerk
turns.text: Representative Anyanonu? Present. Representative Borrego? Present. Representative Block? Present. Representative Madalena? Present. Representative Martinez? Present. Representative Reeb? Present. Representative Szczepanski? Present. Representative Tejada? Present. Representative Zamora? Present. Madam Chair, we have a quorum.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: We have a quorum. You may begin, Representative.

turns.speaker: Rep. Harry Garcia
turns.text:
Thank you, Madam Chair and committee members. My name is Harry Garcia from District 69. Today I am here to ask you to take a look at House Memorial 64, sponsored by myself and Chair Lente. This memorial requests the Indian Affairs Department to provide technical assistance capacity to the state's Indian nations, tribes, and pueblos to promote effective and timely use of capital outlay project funds. As the legislature considers changes to capital outlay rules, this will help our communities. 

Madam Chair and members, as you recall, Chair Lente proposed House Bill 247 which changed some of the capital outlay rules. In the interim, we heard from many tribal communities about the significant challenges they face with different capital outlay systems. This memorial encourages the Indian Affairs Department to fill three positions that are currently open to assist our tribal communities with capital outlay projects. We are hopeful this will be a solution. If HB 247 is implemented, tribal nations and pueblos will have about 10 months to start spending down based on the criteria listed to maintain their appropriations. If they aren't able to meet that criteria, the funds go to a tribal infrastructure fund where they have to apply all over again in a very competitive process. So again, this is a memorial to request assistance and support services from the Indian Affairs Department. Thank you.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Thank you. Let's go to the audience. Do we have anyone who wishes to speak in support or opposition? Not seeing any, let's go to the webcast. No one on the webcast? I will go to the committee. Vice Chair Tejada.

turns.speaker: Vice Chair Rep. Janelle Tejada
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you, Representative, for bringing this forward. We have problems throughout the state everywhere we look with how capital outlay money is being handled. When you were doing your investigation and studying how you were going to approach this, did you come across anything that you thought might be a bill you could put together for the whole state instead? Or are your issues within Indian Affairs uniquely different than the rest of the state?

turns.speaker: Rep. Harry Garcia
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair and Representative. I was solely focused on the way that our capital outlay allocations from this body flow to the Indian Affairs Department and then down to our communities. I do understand that there are issues elsewhere. I spoke with a representative yesterday about this regarding our smaller acequia groups and land grant groups. However, at the moment, I do not have them included in this memorial because I believe they go through DFA directly and they have other sources of support. This here is specific to the tribal entities—pueblos and nations—that have to go through the Indian Affairs Department.

turns.speaker: Vice Chair Rep. Janelle Tejada
turns.text: Okay. I just think sometimes when we concentrate on one area, we should be concentrating on the whole state and how we fix our issues, but I look forward to the rest of the debate. Thank you, Madam Chair.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Thank you. Representative Borrego.

turns.speaker: Rep. Cynthia Borrego
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. Yeah, I had similar questions. During the debate on Chairman Lente's bill, I actually voted against it because I had many of these questions. I didn't know about this memorial then. I support this memorial due to the issues I have with how quickly those changes take effect. Can you go over those changes a little bit more specifically as they relate to your memorial?

turns.speaker: Rep. Harry Garcia
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair and Representative. If you look at House Bill 247, page seven, there are requirements set forth regarding unexpended money and how it will be reverted. Some of the rules are that projects may only be authorized once for a period of two years, or three years if at least 10% of the initial appropriation has been encumbered by the first day of January in which the request for reauthorization is made. Reauthorization may not alter the original purpose, although technical changes are permitted. Capital outlay authorizations for projects in excess of $100,000 must appear on a capital improvement plan. Those are the challenges. That is why I described the 10-month window as a challenge that most tribes will face when this is signed into law.

turns.speaker: Rep. Cynthia Borrego
turns.text: I agree with you. I debated this bill on the floor and I understand we might work on something to address the entire state, but you're focused on this because of how the capital amount goes into a different plan, and then that plan becomes competitive again with no assurance of reallocation. There's an extra step. I stand behind you on this; it supports the arguments I had on the floor. We need to come back next year and work on a broader scope of that along with the chairman. We are moving in the right direction as far as reorganizing how we get to a final place for capital outlay so that we are more fiscally responsible, but I don't want those communities that are in most need to be harmed. Thank you.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Thank you. Representative Tejada.

turns.speaker: Vice Chair Rep. Janelle Tejada
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. I'd like to move a do-pass.

turns.speaker: Rep. John Block
turns.text: Second.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: We have a motion for a do-pass by Vice Chair Tejada, and a second by Ranking Member Block. Do we have any opposition? Seeing none, please show all present voting in the affirmative. You have a do-pass. All righty, I see Representative Torres Velasquez is here for House Memorial 65. Good morning, Representative. Welcome to our committee. Anytime you're ready.

turns.speaker: Rep. Torres Velasquez
turns.text:
Thank you, Madam Chair and members of the committee. This is House Memorial 65. It requests that the Department of Health work together with the Department of Public Safety to convene a task force and conduct research on health and firearm safety policies. The goal is to make recommendations on how to reduce firearm violence in New Mexico. This memorial came about after many meetings with the community and concerns raised by my constituents during my campaign. There are families affected by gun violence and families afraid to send their children to school because of mass shootings. Rather than looking at this as gun control versus gun rights, the group suggested looking at it as a health issue and a safety issue. 

One of the leaders in our group is a doctor and a geriatric specialist. He noted that at medical association conferences, gun violence and gun deaths are consistently identified as top health issues. We can look at this as a public health issue: studying patterns and causes of death. In our research, we found there are approximately 330 million guns in our country and 40,000 deaths. How is it that hundreds of millions of guns are kept safely, but some are used in ways that cause harm? We believe the Department of Health and Public Safety can look for patterns and creative solutions. It's similar to how we looked at car safety in the 60s and 70s. We didn't stop production of cars, but we introduced seatbelts, air cushions, and car seats that saved thousands of lives. We can do the same with firearm safety. This comes from people in House District 30 and neighboring communities. There is strong interest in collecting data and doing research.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Let's go to the audience. Do we have any supporting comments or opposition? We'll start with the webcast. Kathleen Mosley.

turns.speaker: Kathleen Mosley
turns.text: Madam Chair and members of the committee, thank you for hearing this, and thank you, Representative Torres Velasquez, for bringing House Memorial 65 forward. I'm a retired community health and public health nurse and a gun violence survivor. My professor in my public health program was murdered in one of our country's earliest mass school shooting incidents, which left six dead. According to the K-12 school shooting database, there have been almost 3,000 school shooting incidents in the US. In New Mexico, we are not immune, experiencing over two dozen incidents in that same timeframe. 4% of New Mexico high school children interviewed through the Youth Risk Behavior Survey said they brought a weapon to school in the last 30 days. House Memorial 65 places firearm safety in the purview of the health department and public safety department to make plans with a public health approach using data, policy, and community-based programs. It takes courage to initiate action that keeps people safe given that there are guns in the environment. This gives the departments space to work together on a long-term plan. Thank you.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Thank you. Dr. Heather Wood? You have one minute for public comment.

turns.speaker: Dr. Heather Wood
turns.text: Good morning, Madam Chair, committee, and Representative Torres Velasquez. Thank you for bringing this memorial. I am in support of House Memorial 65. I'm a family medicine doctor in Albuquerque and gun violence is absolutely a health safety issue. My husband is an emergency medicine doctor and unfortunately sees way too many gun injuries. Just last year at Rust Medical Center ER, there were shots outside the hospital that went through the walls, injuring patients inside who were seeking care. Just yesterday in Tijeras, there was a domestic shooting that left a four-year-old dead and a one-year-old and a mother in critical condition. It would be such a great step to study this and have a cohesive plan to protect all people. Thank you.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Thank you. Athena Christodoulou.

turns.speaker: Athena Christodoulou
turns.text: I'd like to thank the Chair and the committee for considering this House Memorial and Representative Torres Velasquez. I have seen the effect that a good workgroup can have on future legislation. Because we have a major problem with gun violence, I hope and pray that you would pass this memorial. Thank you.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Thank you. Committee members? Representative Block.

turns.speaker: Rep. John Block
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you, Representative. I'm not adverse to this idea. I'm wondering how we selected the individuals for the task force. One concern I have is that there isn't someone from a gun safety training perspective. In my community, we have stakeholders like gun range instructors, folks from Project Appleseed, and the NRA who do courses in gun safety. I would love to have those voices. Were they considered?

turns.speaker: Rep. Torres Velasquez
turns.text: Excellent question, Representative Block. These are suggestions for who would be on the task force and it is not limited to these ideas. There is certainly room for someone such as those you described. My daughter-in-law just finished her gun safety classes and has wonderful instructors. I recognize those would be very important people to include because they teach gun safety and how to use a firearm properly. Absolutely, let's put someone from that area on here as well.

turns.speaker: Rep. John Block
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair and Representative. I appreciate that. I see 'community and prevention stakeholders' which may vaguely cover them, but I'd like to see it explicitly. On page four, we talk about police chiefs of Albuquerque, Las Cruces, or Santa Fe. Have we considered other places in more rural areas or even tribal areas?

turns.speaker: Rep. Torres Velasquez
turns.text: Yes, absolutely, Madam Chair and Representative. We want to make sure that's clear and that it actually comes to be. Guns are everywhere, and we want to work with constituents in rural areas as much as the cities.

turns.speaker: Rep. John Block
turns.text: I appreciate that. One more question on page three. There is a 'whereas' that says 'as global temperatures rise because of climate change, suicide rates are also likely to rise.' How did we get to that? If we are targeting firearm safety, does rising temperature have to do with firearms? I'm not sure I see the correlation.

turns.speaker: Rep. Torres Velasquez
turns.text: I believe two-thirds of deaths by gun violence are suicides. One correlation appearing in the data is that when living conditions are hard in terms of extreme temperature, sometimes people resort to taking their own lives. It was intended to capture issues starting to come up in the data. We've been working on this for some time, and we also want to look at supporting veterans in terms of gun safety as well.

turns.speaker: Rep. John Block
turns.text: I haven't seen the data on that, so I wouldn't feel comfortable voting on this right now. I know in places like Washington State or England where there is a lack of sunlight, there are high suicide rates. But in New Mexico, we have so much sunlight. I'm not sure about the temperature argument. I appreciate you taking this on because I want to end violence in our communities and prevent suicide. I have many veterans in my district. I also love guns—I want to make sure people are owning them safely and doing courses so they have the skills ready. Because of the data I haven't seen regarding climate and the need for tighter language on rural and safety stakeholders, I can't support it at this time, but I thank you for the conversation.

turns.speaker: Rep. Torres Velasquez
turns.text: Yes, Madam Chair. One of the assumptions is that gun deaths are correlated with mental health, and that relates to the question on suicide. Looking at the data would be very helpful in identifying patterns and solutions. Being in my first term, I am still learning, but I'm happy to provide websites with more data. We aren't really collecting or using this process yet in the state, and that is what we want to start doing.

turns.speaker: Rep. Cynthia Borrego
turns.text: The purpose of the task force would be to get more information and data specifically for New Mexico. We know we are among the highest states for gun-related suicides. I think you are hitting on an important point. In the second judicial district, there is a task force related to this that we just gave funding to through the budget. It's held in the DA's office. You are on the same track as what is already going on. I support you.

turns.speaker: Vice Chair Rep. Janelle Tejada
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair and Representative, for bringing this forward. I think this will open opportunities for more information. I am pleased to see research to study the connection between alcohol use and gun violence, and the suicide rate which is a huge loss for rural communities. As a member of a family affected by gun violence, this is very important to me. My question is about the timeline. I see the end date for the report is September 15th. What are your expected meeting times and how will the group be assembled?

turns.speaker: Rep. Torres Velasquez
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair and Madam Vice Chair. I am joined by Senator Pinto, who has proposed similar memorials. For the timeline, we thought monthly meetings would be appropriate. The September 15th deadline was set so the report could be given to the interim health committee so they can start making suggestions for the next session.

turns.speaker: Rep. Anyanonu
turns.text: Madam Chair, I move a do-pass.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: We have a motion for a do-pass and a second. Clerk, please call the roll.

turns.speaker: Committee Clerk
turns.text: Representative Anyanonu? Yes. Representative Block? No. Representative Borrego? Yes. Representative Madalena? Yes. Representative Martinez? No. Representative Szczepanski? Yes. Representative Zamora? No.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson
turns.text: Madam Chair, you have four in the affirmative and three in the negative. The motion passes. You have a do-pass on your memorial. Good morning, Senator Pinto. Committee members, we are adjourned.

[Chair Rep. D. Wonda Johnson]: Madam Chair, you have four in the affirmative and three in the negative. The motion passes. You have a do-pass on your memorial. Good morning, Senator Pinto. Committee members, we are adjourned.
